items.title: Evolution of Vascular Access for Hemodialysis
items.content: The development of vascular access for hemodialysis has evolved significantly since the first human treatments in 1924. Early methods involved using glass needles and rotating drum kidneys, which were limited by repetitive access challenges. The introduction of arteriovenous fistulas (AVFs) and advancements in graft materials have improved long-term dialysis access.
keyPoints: First hemodialysis treatments in humans performed in 1924 using glass needles.
Rotating drum kidney used in 1943, limited by repetitive vascular access challenges.
Development of arteriovenous Teflon shunt and Cimino fistula (side-to-side-anastomoses between radial artery and cephalic vein).
Catheter-based approaches for RRT designed initially in 1961 for hemodialysis, 1968 for peritoneal dialysis.
Introduction of modified bovine carotid artery grafts in 1972, ePTFE grafts in 1976, and tissue-engineered blood vessels from human fibroblasts and endothelial cells in 2016.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.title: Technological Advancements in Dialysis Access
items.content: Advances in material sciences have led to the development of various grafts for hemodialysis, including ePTFE and tissue-engineered vessels. Additionally, high-pressure angioplasty balloons and Nitinol stents are used for managing vascular access complications.
keyPoints: ePTFE grafts introduced in 1976, some modified with silicone for early cannulation.
Ultra-high-pressure (up to 40 atm) angioplasty balloons used in peripheral and central venous therapy.
Nitinol self-expanding stents and stent grafts as rescue tools for unsuccessful angioplasty or vessel rupture.
Role of drug-coated balloons in dialysis access care being assessed, with mixed clinical trial results limiting widespread use.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.title: Optimal Placement and Complications of Peritoneal Dialysis Catheters
items.content: Proper placement of peritoneal dialysis catheter cuffs is crucial for function and minimizing complications. The internal cuff should be within the rectus sheath, while the external cuff should be 2–4 cm from the skin exit site.
keyPoints: Internal cuff placement: within the rectus sheath just laterally to the linea alba
External cuff placement: 2–4 cm from the skin exit site
Omental entrapment may require laparoscopic intervention; omentopexy can prevent this
Repeated infections and exposure to glucose-containing solutions affect peritoneal membrane permeability
Encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis is a late-stage complication triggered by repeated peritonitis
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.title: Management of Hemodialysis Catheter Complications
items.content: Catheter-related bacteremia and thrombotic occlusions are significant complications in hemodialysis. Treatment involves catheter exchange or removal, with specific interventions for fibrinous sheaths and central vein stenosis.
keyPoints: Polyurethane dialysis catheters allow blood flow rates of 400–500 mL/min
Tunneled catheters have cuffs to prevent infection between skin flora and the sterile tunnel
Catheter-related bacteremia: treated with catheter exchange or removal
Thrombotic occlusion leads to sclerotic scarring, potentially causing central vein stenosis if contact time >3 months
Clinically significant pulmonary embolism from catheter-associated thrombus is rare; intra-atrial thrombus >2 cm may require intervention
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.title: Evolution and Challenges of Arteriovenous Access in Hemodialysis
items.content: The shift from arteriovenous grafts to fistulas reflects changes in patient demographics and disease prevalence. Despite campaigns promoting fistula use, challenges remain due to vascular conditions.
keyPoints: Historically, forearm Cimino AVFs were common; now 65% of dialysis patients use AVFs
AVG prevalence decreased from 65% to <20% due to 'Fistula First' campaigns
75% of AVFs are now created in the upper arm due to larger veins and arteries
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.title: Optimal Flow Requirements for Dialysis Access
items.content: Successful dialysis requires an AVF or AVG to provide sufficient blood flow, specifically at least the desired pump speed plus 100–200 mL/min. This minimizes recirculation and prevents access collapse.
keyPoints: Desired flow in the United States is typically 600–800 mL/min.
A fistula with an artery ≥2 mm diameter and a vein ≥3 mm will have >500 mL/min flow when systolic BP >100 mmHg.
AVF flow increases to 800 mL/min within weeks, while AVG flow can reach 1000 mL/min within minutes after creation.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.title: Chronic Dialysis Access Disease and Stenosis Management
items.content: Long-term dialysis access is prone to chronic disease, with AVGs developing recurrent venous anastomotic stenoses every 3-4 months. Stent grafts can extend patency for about a year.
keyPoints: AVGs require re-angioplasty one to three times per year due to fibrous depositions at the stent edges.
Forearm radial-cephalic autogenous accesses are prone to low flow from juxta-anastomotic stenoses.
Upper arm brachial-cephalic accesses develop accelerated stenosis recurrence after angioplasty.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.title: Complications of High-Access Flow
items.content: High-access flow (>1500 mL/min) can lead to systemic complications such as heart failure and pulmonary hypertension, as well as local issues like aneurysm formation and steal syndrome.
keyPoints: Fistula inflow exceeding outflow capacity increases intra-access pressures beyond 20–35 mmHg.
High-access flows are associated with ischemia of the hand (steal syndrome).
Banding is a common procedure to reduce access flow by creating an inflow stenosis.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Pulsatility in Dialysis Access
items.content: Pulsatility during the physical examination of dialysis access reflects the force of expansion during systole and softening during diastole. Increased pulsatility suggests high blood pressure or an outflow stenosis, while reduced softening indicates potential issues with the access.
keyPoints: Increased pulsatility can suggest very high blood pressures or an outflow stenosis.
Reduced softening during diastole is indicative of an outflow stenosis.
An inflow stenosis blunts the systolic component and may create a sensation of an 'empty' access unless there's a coexisting outflow stenosis.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.title: Interpreting Flow Murmurs in Dialysis Access
items.content: Flow murmurs during dialysis access examination can indicate the presence and severity of stenoses. A change in pitch towards higher frequency suggests a stenosis, while a discontinuous flow murmur indicates severe inflow or outflow stenosis.
keyPoints: A change in pitch toward higher frequency is typical at the site of a stenosis due to accelerated flow velocity.
A discontinuous flow murmur signifies very low diastolic flow, indicative of severe stenosis.
The stenotic inflow murmur is faint (like a whistle), whereas the outflow murmur can be coarse and loud.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.title: Assessing Augmentation in Dialysis Access
items.content: Augmentation, or engorgement of the access body with occlusion of the outflow, is crucial for safe needle insertions. Impaired augmentation can result from inflow stenosis, side branches, or collaterals.
keyPoints: Inflow stenosis impairs augmentation necessary for successful needle insertions.
The location of branches affecting augmentation can be identified by moving the occluding finger closer to the anastomosis until augmentation is achieved.
Collapse of the access with arm elevation indicates inflow and outflow capacity match or mismatch, with forearm accesses typically showing complete collapse.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.title: Why Preserve Upper Arm Veins in CKD Patients?
items.content: In patients with advancing chronic kidney disease (CKD), preserving upper arm veins is crucial due to the potential need for future autogenous access. The use of cardiac rhythm management devices (CRMDs) can lead to loss of these veins, necessitating careful planning.
keyPoints: 8–10% of CKD patients may require CRMDs, risking upper arm cephalic vein loss and central venous stenoses.
Plan future autogenous access sites considering the location of any existing CRMD.
Avoid PICCs in CKD stage 3 or higher; use jugular vein tunneled catheters to preserve arm veins for long-term access.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.title: How to Manage Dialysis Access in Kidney Transplant Patients?
items.content: The approach to dialysis access in kidney transplant patients should consider the transplanted kidney's function, risk of disease recurrence, and potential benefits for blood pressure control.
keyPoints: Dialysis access strategy depends on transplanted kidney function.
Consider the risk of kidney disease recurrence in the transplant.
Balance access flow to maintain patency while limiting it over time.
Evaluate the AVF's role in managing blood pressure.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.question: Which year marked the introduction of a modified bovine carotid artery biological graft for hemodialysis?
items.explanation: The passage states that a modified bovine carotid artery biological graft was introduced in 1972.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.question: What material is used to modify some ePTFE grafts for early cannulation?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that some ePTFE grafts are modified with a silicone layer to allow for early cannulation.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.question: What is the maximum pressure up to which angioplasty balloons can be used in peripheral and central venous therapy?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that ultra-high-pressure angioplasty balloons are used up to 40 atm.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended location for the internal cuff of a peritoneal dialysis catheter?
options: Within the rectus sheath just laterally to the linea alba
2–4 cm from the skin exit site
In the subcutaneous tissue near the abdominal wall
Directly on the surface of the abdomen
items.explanation: The internal cuff is placed within the rectus sheath just laterally to the linea alba.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.question: What can be done if a peritoneal dialysis catheter becomes encased in a fibrinous sheath that limits fluid flow?
options: Guidewire manipulation to disrupt the sheath
items.explanation: The fibrinous sheath can be disrupted by guidewire manipulation if it limits fluid flow.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.question: What is a common complication of long-term peritoneal dialysis related to the peritoneal membrane?
items.explanation: Encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis is a late-stage complication of PD thought to be triggered by repeated peritonitis.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.question: What is the typical material used for dialysis catheters and its characteristic at body temperature?
items.explanation: Dialysis catheters are typically made of polyurethane that softens at body temperature.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.question: What is the typical flow volume range for mature upper arm AVFs?
items.explanation: Mature upper arm AVFs typically have flow volumes of 1400–1800 mL/min, but can be as high as 2000–4000 mL/min.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.question: What is the typical flow volume range for AVGs after access creation?
items.explanation: After access creation, the flow volume in AVGs increases to the range of 1200–1800 mL/min.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.question: Which type of AVF is most prone to low flow due to juxta-anastomotic stenoses?
items.explanation: Forearm radial-cephalic autogenous accesses of the Cimino type at the wrist are most prone to low flow due to juxta-anastomotic stenoses.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.question: What is a common procedure used to reduce high-access flows in dialysis access?
items.explanation: The most common procedure to reduce high-access flows is banding, where a Prolene suture creates an inflow stenosis.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.question: Which physical examination finding suggests a severe inflow or outflow stenosis in dialysis access?
options: An increase in pulsatility during diastole
items.explanation: A discontinuous flow murmur indicates severe inflow or outflow stenosis as it signifies low flow during diastole.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.question: What does an increased pulsatility and reduced softening during diastole indicate in dialysis access examination?
items.explanation: Increased pulsatility and reduced softening during diastole are indicative of an outflow stenosis.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.question: What is the implication of a continuous thrill in dialysis access examination?
options: Well-developed access usually in the inflow segment
Complete collapse of upper arm access
Focal thrill immediately after a stenosis
items.explanation: A continuous thrill typically indicates a well-developed access, often found in the inflow segment.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.question: What does complete collapse of a forearm access with arm elevation indicate?
items.explanation: Complete collapse of a forearm access indicates an inflow and outflow capacity match.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.question: Which vein is at risk of being lost due to cardiac rhythm management devices (CRMDs)?
items.explanation: The upper arm cephalic vein can be lost due to the placement of CRMDs.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.question: What type of catheter is recommended for CKD stage 3 or higher patients to preserve arm veins?
options: Tunneled small-diameter catheter in the internal jugular vein
Central venous catheter in the femoral vein
items.explanation: Using a tunneled small-diameter catheter in the internal jugular vein preserves arm veins for long-term access creation.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.question: What should be considered when choosing arterial access points for cardiac procedures?
items.explanation: Arterial access points should be chosen with arteriovenous fistula (AVF) creation in mind.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.statement: Cimino fistula involves a side-to-side anastomosis between the radial artery and the cephalic vein at the wrist.
items.explanation: The passage describes the Cimino fistula as involving a side-to-side-anastomoses between the radial artery and the cephalic vein at the wrist.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.statement: Drug-coated balloons are widely used in dialysis access care due to their cost-effectiveness.
items.explanation: The passage states that drug-coated balloons have mixed clinical trial results and greater costs, limiting widespread use.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Interventional Nephrology | HISTORY

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.statement: Omental entrapment of a peritoneal dialysis catheter can often be prevented by omentopexy at the time of catheter placement.
items.explanation: Omentopexy at the time of PD catheter placement can prevent later omental entrapment.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.statement: Catheter-related bacteremia is best treated with antibiotics alone.
items.explanation: Catheter-related bacteremia is best treated with exchange of the catheter and disruption of any fibrinous sheath.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.statement: High-access flow can lead to systemic complications such as heart failure and pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: High-access flows (>1500 mL/min) are associated with systemic complications like heart failure and pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.statement: Forearm AVFs typically have higher flow volumes than upper arm AVFs.
items.explanation: Forearm AVFs usually have lower flow volumes (500–700 mL/min) compared to upper arm AVFs.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.statement: A change in pitch toward higher frequency during the physical examination is typical at the site of a stenosis.
items.explanation: Higher frequency changes are typical due to accelerated flow velocity at the site of a stenosis.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.statement: An inflow stenosis will enhance augmentation during physical examination.
items.explanation: Inflow stenosis impairs augmentation, not enhances it.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.statement: PICCs are recommended for CKD stage 3 or higher patients to preserve arm veins.
items.explanation: PICCs should be avoided in CKD stage 3 or higher patients; instead, tunneled small-diameter catheters in the internal jugular vein are recommended.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.statement: Planning for future autogenous access and CRMD location is not necessary.
items.explanation: It is recommended to plan for which side a future autogenous access is to be placed and where a CRMD is located in all cases.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.assertion: Catheter vessel wall contact points can lead to central vein stenosis.
items.reason: Central vein stenosis is more commonly observed in patients with catheter contact times of longer duration (>3 months).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)
items.assertion: Catheter tip position in large central veins is ideal for dialysis treatments.
items.reason: The catheter tip should be positioned in the right atrium to avoid injury from dynamic blood movement during dialysis treatments.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; catheter tips should ideally be in the right atrium.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.assertion: Increased flows and pressure in the venous segment of the access circuit lead to chronic dialysis access disease.
items.reason: AVGs develop recurrent venous anastomotic stenoses due to shear stress and needle insertion-related injury.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why increased flows and pressure lead to chronic dialysis access disease.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)
items.assertion: Stent grafts in AVGs can extend patency indefinitely without further intervention.
items.reason: Stent grafts require re-angioplasty one to three times per year due to buildup of pauci-cellular fibrous depositions at the stent edges.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because stent grafts do not extend patency indefinitely; they require periodic interventions.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DIALYSIS ACCESS (part 2)

items.assertion: A discontinuous flow murmur is a sign of severe inflow or outflow stenosis.
items.reason: During diastole, the flow is so low that no audible shear force is created.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.assertion: An isolated thrill found immediately after a stenosis is always indicative of severe pathology.
items.reason: A focal thrill can be present without indicating severe pathology if there's no change in pulsatility at the site.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as an isolated thrill does not always indicate severe pathology.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · Physical Examination of Dialysis Access

items.assertion: Avoiding PICCs helps preserve arm veins for long-term access creation.
items.reason: CKD patients often require intravenous access beyond hospital stays, necessitating preservation of arm veins.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why avoiding PICCs helps preserve arm veins.
items.reference: Interventional Nephrology · PRESERVATION OF VENOUS "REAL ESTATE"
